title: Spinal Dysraphisms
authors: Nguyen Q, Tomei K / Fanaroff & Martin 2025

blocks.text:
ทำไม neonatologist ต้องเข้าใจ Spinal Dysraphisms?
NTDs (neural tube defects) เป็น birth defect อันดับ 2 รองจาก CHD — พบ 1–2/1,000 pregnancies worldwide. (1) Myelomeningocele (MMC) = 50% ของ NTDs ทั้งหมด: เราต้องรักษาใน NICU ตั้งแต่ minute 1 — ปิดแผล, ป้องกัน meningitis, monitor hydrocephalus; (2) Cutaneous stigmata เป็น clue สำคัญของ closed dysraphism — รู้จัก high-risk vs low-risk → imaging ได้ถูกต้อง → prevent tethered cord; (3) MOMS trial (NEJM 2011): prenatal repair ลด shunt rate จาก 82% → 40% — เราต้องรู้ว่าทารกที่มา postnatal มี background อะไร; (4) 60–80% ของ MMC → hydrocephalus + VP shunt → Chiari II malformation → apnea/bradycardia → นี่คือ emergency ที่ต้องวินิจฉัยเร็ว; (5) Latex allergy: ใช้ nonlatex gloves ทุกครั้งที่ touch ทารก MMC — sensitization ป้องกันได้.
เป้าหมายการเรียนรู้วันนี้
อธิบาย embryology ของ spinal dysraphisms: gastrulation, primary neurulation, disjunction, secondary neurulation — failure ของแต่ละขั้น → dysraphism ชนิดใด
จำแนก open vs closed spinal dysraphisms พร้อม subtypes ของแต่ละกลุ่ม (Table 60.1)
อธิบาย pathology ของ meningocele, myelomeningocele (cystica vs aperta), lipomyelomeningocele (3 types), dorsal dermal sinus, diastematomyelia, neuroenteric cyst, abnormal filum terminale
ระบุ cutaneous stigmata แบ่ง high/intermediate/low risk และ imaging recommendation ตาม risk category
อธิบาย prenatal screening: MSAFP (timing, cutoff, sensitivity/specificity) + amniocentesis AFP + AChE + fetal US; prevention: folate dose
อธิบาย imaging: US vs MRI — indication, limitation, timing (posterior element ossification <6 mo)
ระบุ functional level assessment ของ MMC (Table 60.3): T10, L1–L5, S1 → motor/sensory landmarks
วางแผน delivery room management ของ open MMC: positioning, sterile dressing, neurologic exam, antibiotic regimen, timing of closure
Monitor + manage hydrocephalus และ Chiari II malformation symptoms ใน perioperative period
สรุป MOMS trial (Adzick NEJM 2011) + MOMS II (Houtrow Pediatrics 2020): endpoints, key findings, limitations; two-hit theory ของ fetal repair benefit
อธิบาย surgical approaches: postnatal repair (standard of care) vs open fetal surgery vs fetoscopic repair — risks + learning curve
วางแผน closed dysraphism management: indications for urgent vs elective surgery, tethered cord syndrome, retethering risk
1. Embryology ของ Spinal Dysraphisms
1.1 4 Processes ที่ Failure → Dysraphism
Gastrulation: bilaminar → trilaminar disc (mesoderm, endoderm, ectoderm); neuroenteric canal เกิดชั่วคราวระหว่าง gastrulation → failure of resolution → split cord malformation + neuroenteric cysts

Primary neurulation: notochord induces neuroectoderm → neural plate → neural plate folds → neural groove closure → neural tube; initiated at multiple closure sites, proceeds bidirectionally (zipper-like); failure → open NTDs (MMC, meningocele) at any level

Disjunction: neural tube separates from surface ectoderm; mesoderm migrates between → meninges, vertebrae, skull, paraspinal muscles; premature disjunction → lipomyelomeningocele; incomplete disjunction → dermal sinus

Secondary neurulation: forms spinal cord caudal to mid-sacral region; multipotent tail bud cells → canalization → secondary neural tube; failure → lower sacral/coccygeal dysraphisms (tethered cord via faulty tissue separation; multi-germ-layer cell types = multipotential tail bud origin)
1.2 Signaling Pathways + Environmental/Genetic Risk Factors
Pathways involved: planar cell polarity (PCP), SHH, BMP, grainyhead-like (GRHL), retinoid signaling (RA) + apoptosis + cellular proliferation
Environmental teratogens: valproic acid; carbamazepine; trimethoprim; fumonisin (fungal product); folate/B12 deficiency; inositol; maternal diabetes mellitus
Genetic: MTHFR C677T + A1298C polymorphisms → 1.8-fold ↑ NTD risk; several other modifier genes (GRHL3, VANGL1, VANGL2, CELSR1 — PCP pathway)
2. Classification ของ Spinal Dysraphisms

Key Distinction: Open vs Closed
Open dysraphisms: neural tissue/CSF directly exposed → ↑ meningitis risk → urgent closure within 24–48h + prophylactic antibiotics. Closed dysraphisms: covered by skin → no direct infection risk (exception: dermal sinus with thecal extension) → delayed, elective surgery unless neurologic deficit develops. Both can cause tethered cord syndrome with progressive neurologic deterioration if untreated.
3. Open Spinal Dysraphisms
3.1 Meningocele
Classified by location: posterior lumbar, sacral, thoracic; posterior cervical; limited dorsal myeloschisis; anterior sacral

Pathology: meninges herniate through posterior vertebral arch defect; spinal cord + nerve roots remain in spinal canal — normal structure and position; lesion pedunculated, transilluminates well, may be covered by skin
Neurologic outcome: generally better than MMC because cord is not exposed
3.2 Myelomeningocele (MMC) — 50% of All NTDs
Spina bifida cystica: open spinal cord covered by meningeal sac
Spina bifida aperta: spinal cord directly exposed without meningeal covering (most morbid)
Neurologic deficits below lesion level: inability to ambulate, bladder/bowel incontinence, hydrocephalus (~60–80%), scoliosis, GI disorders
Chiari II malformation: nearly always present with lumbar MMC → hindbrain herniation → brainstem compression → nystagmus, apnea, swallowing difficulties, hemodynamic instability
Location: ~75% lumbar region
3.3 Functional Level Assessment (Table 60.3)

Practical clues: hip flexion → higher lumbar (L1); knee extension → mid-lumbar (L3); isolated club feet → low-lumbar; functional level ≠ always anatomic level — exposed neural tissue may retain some function → functional level more important for prognosis
4. Closed Spinal Dysraphisms
4.1 Spina Bifida Occulta
Mildest form: vertebrae split at spinous process level; cord + nerve roots intact and in place; defect covered by skin
Associated cutaneous stigmata: midline dimple, hair tuft, skin color change, sinus tract, hemangiomatous lesion
Often asymptomatic for years; late presentation with neurologic compromise if associated with other closed dysraphisms
4.2 Lipomyelomeningocele
Complex closed dysraphism, usually lumbar/sacral region; subcutaneous lipoma extends through lumbosacral fascia + vertebral arches + dura → attaches to spinal cord as tethering mass
3 Types (by anatomic attachment):
Dorsal: lipoma attaches to dorsal cord at level of myeloschisis (neural placode)
Transitional: extends beyond myeloschisis down to conus medullaris + may involve nerve roots
Caudal (chaotic): arises from caudal end of conus medullaris, often encasing nerve roots
Key stat: 48% neurologically intact at initial diagnosis (diagnosed early because fatty mass visible at birth) → prophylactic surgery consideration

Complications: neurogenic bladder/bowel, orthopedic deformities, scoliosis, spasticity; surgical complication rate 10–30%; 5.8% worse neurologic function after surgery; 10–20% retethering postoperatively (Sarris et al Neurosurg Focus 2012)
4.3 Dorsal Dermal Sinus
Epithelium-lined tract, variable depth, usually terminates in or on thecal sac; seen at extremes of neuraxis
Risk: life-threatening meningitis — bacteria track directly into CSF space; also associated with tethered cord, diastematomyelia, inclusion cysts
Despite benign external appearance → timely surgical treatment to prevent infection + neurologic deficit
4.4 Diastematomyelia (Split Cord Malformation)
Spinal cord split into 2 hemicords, each with own ventral + dorsal nerve roots; may share single dural sac or have split dural sac
Fibrous or bony septum may be present between hemicords; conus medullaris often low-lying + tethered by thickened filum terminale
Associated findings: butterfly vertebra, widened spinal canal, fused pedicles, failure of vertebral body segmentation; often co-occurs with MMC and neuroenteric cysts
Embryology: errors in gastrulation (persistent neuroenteric canal) → this is why diastematomyelia + neuroenteric cysts frequently co-occur
4.5 Neuroenteric Cysts
Arise from persistent neuroenteric canal (gastrulation error); most frequent at lower cervical/upper thoracic and conus medullaris
Can be extra- or intradural; if intradural: anterior, posterior, or within spinal cord
Often associated with split cord malformation
4.6 Abnormal Filum Terminale
Mildest closed dysraphism; filum thickened when >2 mm diameter, increased connective tissue or fat, low-lying conus medullaris
Risk of tethered cord syndrome with neurogenic bladder or lower extremity issues if untreated
5. Cutaneous Stigmata — Recognition และ Risk Stratification
5.1 ทำไม Skin Matters
Skin + nervous system share intimate embryologic origin → cutaneous stigmata of closed dysraphisms found in 4.2–7.2% of all neonates with closed spina bifida
2 or more cutaneous stigmata → high predictive value for closed dysraphism
High suspicion also warranted when cutaneous stigmata + anogenital/urogenital malformations
5.2 Risk Categories + Imaging Recommendations (Table 60.2)

rows: Lipoma; acrochordon; pseudotail; aplasia cutis; dermoid cyst/sinus; IH >2.5 cm; ≥2 stigmata
Atypical dimple (>5 mm or >2.5 cm from anal verge); IH <2.5 cm; hypertrichosis
US if <6 mo; MRI if >6 mo
Simple dimple; hyper/hypopigmentation; melanocytic nevi; teratoma; port-wine stain; telangiectasias

blocks.text: IH = infantile hemangioma; atypical dimple = >5 mm diameter หรือ >2.5 cm from anal verge

Clinical Clue — Simple Dimple vs Atypical Dimple
Simple dimple (low risk): <5 mm diameter, <2.5 cm from anal verge, visible base → observation only. Atypical dimple (intermediate risk): >5 mm diameter OR >2.5 cm from anal verge OR non-visible base → ultrasound (if <6 mo). Dermal sinus (high risk): tract with depth into thecal sac → MRI urgently; risk of meningitis if infection tracks into CSF. Bottom line: most sacral dimples are benign, but examine carefully before reassuring.
6. Prenatal Screening และ Prevention
6.1 Alpha-Fetoprotein (AFP) Screening
Timing: maternal serum AFP (MSAFP) at 15–21 weeks gestation; values converted to MoM (multiples of median) to account for gestational age trend
Cutoff: 2.0–2.5 MoM standard; using 2.0 MoM → ↑ detection of open spina bifida by 10%; specificity for open spina bifida: 90%; sensitivity for anencephaly: 100%
Amniocentesis: amniotic fluid AFP ↑ + positive acetylcholinesterase (AChE) confirmation test → detects 96% of open spina bifida
Fetal ultrasound: sensitivity 97%, specificity 100% in experienced hands (Wilson JOGC 2014)

Fetal US anatomy survey (2nd trimester): visualize longitudinal, sagittal, coronal, axial images of spine; localize conus medullaris; measure sac (important if prenatal repair planned — larger defects may need tissue matrix for closure)
6.2 Prevention — Folate
General population: periconceptional folic acid 0.4 mg/day → 70% of NTDs preventable (Kibar et al Clin Genet 2007)
High-risk (one affected child already): 4 mg/day of folic acid before and during subsequent pregnancies
Risk also ↑ with: excessive retinoic acid/vitamin A exposure; MTHFR polymorphisms (C677T + A1298C → 1.8× risk); valproic acid, carbamazepine
7. Imaging ของ Spinal Dysraphisms
7.1 Spinal Ultrasonography
Possible in newborns + early infancy (<6 months): posterior elements predominantly cartilaginous → acoustic window exists; quality ↓ as ossification occurs after 6 months

Uses: localize conus medullaris, identify osseous/soft tissue defects, measure sacs, identify neural elements (linear echogenic areas in anechoic CSF), visualize neural placode (hypoechoic ovoid region within sac)
Limitations: operator dependent; maternal obesity, fetal movement, fetal positioning affect accuracy
7.2 MRI — Gold Standard

Advantages: superior soft-tissue resolution; diagnose complex dysraphisms (rotation of neural elements); evaluate hindbrain herniation pre-repair; distinguishes open vs closed; guides surgical planning
Fetal MRI: indicated when in utero repair being considered; if good-quality prenatal images obtained → may eliminate need for postnatal sedation imaging (Egloff & Bulas Semin Ultrasound CT MR 2015)
Postnatal MRI: requires sedation for quality images; recommended for all high-risk cutaneous findings and all confirmed closed dysraphisms
8. Delivery Room and Perioperative Management
8.1 Mode of Delivery
Some evidence: cesarean section before labor → ↑ motor function at 2 years vs any mode after labor (Greene et al Am J Obstet Gynecol 2016)

Controversy: other studies show no difference once labor commences; must consider maternal risk; obstetrician + neurosurgeon decide based on hydrocephalus severity (large head → may preclude vaginal delivery)
8.2 Immediate Delivery Room Steps — Open Dysraphism

Delivery Room Protocol for Myelomeningocele
Step 1 — Gloves: sterile, NON-LATEX gloves to examine lesion — prevent latex sensitization (Cremer et al Allergy 1998). Step 2 — Cover lesion: sterile saline-soaked dressing; larger defects → plastic wrap to prevent heat loss. Step 3 — Position: prone or lateral decubitus — avoid pressure on lesion; never supine. Step 4 — Assess CSF leak: note size/location of lesion, leakage of CSF. Step 5 — Neurologic exam: spontaneous activity, muscle weakness extent, leg posture/paralysis, sensation, deep tendon reflexes, anocutaneous reflex, functional level (Table 60.3). Step 6 — Assess for hydrocephalus: OFC, fontanelle, sutures; Chiari II signs (apnea, bradycardia, nystagmus, swallowing difficulties). Step 7 — Antibiotics within 1 hour: ampicillin + gentamicin or cefotaxime (GBS + gram-negative coverage); continue ≥48h after repair.
8.3 Antibiotic Prophylaxis
Regimen: ampicillin + (gentamicin OR cefotaxime) — covers GBS and gram-negative bacteria
Timing: start within 1 hour of birth; continue for ≥48h after repair (Charney et al Am J Dis Child 1991)
Sterility + timely repair → ↓ early CNS infection; prophylactic antibiotics alone not sufficient without wound closure
8.4 Timing of Surgical Closure
Target: within 24–48 hours of birth (most centers advocate <24–48h; no consensus exists beyond this general target)
Surgical repair steps: approximate lateral edges of open neural plate in midline → form neural tube; close meningeal sac; close skin
Post-op position: prone or lateral to protect incision; use bumpers to prevent urine/feces from contaminating wound
8.5 Hydrocephalus and Chiari II Monitoring

Signs and Symptoms to Monitor (Table 60.4)
Hydrocephalus: bulging anterior fontanelle; ↑ head circumference (daily OFC mandatory); sunsetting eyes; dilated scalp veins; irritability; vomiting; Cushing triad. Chiari II Malformation: nystagmus; apnea; gagging; swallowing difficulties; syncope; weakness; lower brainstem hemodynamic instability. Action: apnea/bradycardia + lower brainstem signs → treat hydrocephalus promptly; persistence after shunt = poor prognostic sign. Monitoring schedule: daily head circumference + weekly cranial ultrasound post-MMC repair. 60–80% of MMC infants develop hydrocephalus → VP shunt required (Talamonti et al J Neurosurg 2007).
9. Fetal Repair of Myelomeningocele — MOMS Trial
9.1 Two-Hit Theory
First hit: neurologic injury at moment of failure of neural tube closure
Second hit: neural tissue exposed to intrauterine contents, hydrodynamic pressures, direct trauma → ongoing injury throughout gestation (Hutchins et al Pediatr Pathol Lab Med 1996)
Intrauterine repair → reduces second hit → improved neurologic outcomes → this is the rationale
9.2 MOMS Trial (Adzick NEJM 2011)
Design: RCT, 183 patients, multiple US centers; prenatal repair (<26 wk gestation) vs standard postnatal repair; enrollment stopped early for efficacy
Primary outcomes at 12 months:
Shunt rate: prenatal 40% vs postnatal 82% — major reduction
Mental + motor development at 30 months: significantly better in prenatal group
Secondary outcomes: hindbrain herniation ↓ in prenatal group; ambulation at 30 months ↑ in prenatal group
Limitations: ↑ preterm delivery; uterine dehiscence at delivery; location restrictions → narrow applicable population; limited center availability
9.3 MOMS II Follow-Up (Houtrow Pediatrics 2020)
Primary outcome: Vineland composite score at school age — no significant difference between prenatal vs postnatal groups
However: prenatal group parents reported reduced negative social and family impact + continued benefit in motor/neurologic outcomes from MOMS
9.4 Surgical Techniques for Fetal Repair

Open technique: pubic → umbilical incision; fetal/placental localization by US; 6–8 cm hysterotomy; sharp incision of neural placode → elements fall into spinal canal; watertight dura closure (or synthetic dura); tensionless skin closure (Dewan & Wellons J Neurosurg Pediatr 2019)

Endoscopic (fetoscopic) technique: evolved over time due to limited visualization + small working field; earlier: lay synthetic patch → postnatal skin closure; recent: patch + single-layer skin closure through endoscope; ↑ learning curve; limited to expert centers
Standard of care remains: postnatal repair within 24–48h — fetal repair limited by maternal risk + center expertise
10. NICU Practical
10.1 Closed Dysraphism — When to Act
MRI indication (Table-based): 2+ cutaneous lumbosacral stigmata; subcutaneous back mass; neurologic symptoms of tethered cord
MRI or US (one stigma): isolated midline cutaneous lumbosacral lesion

After radiographic confirmation: refer neurosurgery; surgery non-urgent unless active neurologic deficit; conservative observation appropriate for asymptomatic → but discuss prophylactic surgery given high risk of neurologic deterioration (Copp et al Lancet Neurol 2013)
No prophylactic antibiotics needed for closed dysraphism (exception: dermal sinus with thecal extension)
10.2 Long-Term Follow-Up Plan for MMC
Neurosurgery: VP shunt management, tethered cord release, Chiari II monitoring
Urology: neurogenic bladder — clean intermittent catheterization (CIC), UTI prophylaxis, urodynamic studies
Orthopedics: ambulation planning (depends on lesion level), club foot management, scoliosis monitoring
Developmental pediatrics: cognitive follow-up (shunt function + intelligence); social/family support
11. 5 Bedside Pearls

PEARL 1 — NONLATEX GLOVES: เริ่มตั้งแต่แรกคลอด
ทารก MMC มีความเสี่ยงสูงต่อ latex sensitization — ต้องใช้ nonlatex gloves ทุกครั้งที่สัมผัส ตั้งแต่ delivery room จนตลอดชีวิต (Cremer et al Allergy 1998). Primary latex allergy ป้องกันได้ถ้าไม่มี latex exposure ตั้งแต่ต้น; ถ้า sensitized แล้ว → anaphylaxis risk ระหว่าง surgery. Inform ทุก provider, OR team, ต้องใช้ latex-free environment. นี่เป็น first action ก่อน assess lesion.

PEARL 2 — ดู Functional Level ไม่ใช่ Anatomic Level เพื่อ Predict Ambulation
Functional level ของ MMC สำคัญกว่า anatomic level เพราะ exposed neural tissue บางส่วนยังมี function → underestimate prognosis ถ้า assume anatomic = functional. ประเมินโดย: hip flexion (L1) → higher lumbar; knee extension (L3) → mid-lumbar; isolated club feet → low-lumbar (L4/L5) → ambulation likely possible. S1 function (plantar flexion) = good sign for community ambulation. Re-assess function หลัง closure เมื่อ inflammatory edema ลดลง.

PEARL 3 — Simple Dimple ≠ Emergency; Atypical Dimple = US
Simple sacral dimple (ขนาดเล็ก <5 mm, <2.5 cm จาก anal verge, visible base) = low risk = ไม่ต้อง image. Atypical dimple (>5 mm หรือ >2.5 cm จาก anal verge หรือ non-visible base) = intermediate risk = US ก่อน 6 เดือน. Dermal sinus = high risk = MRI ทันที (risk meningitis). ปัญหาคือหมอ intern มักทำ US ทุก dimple → waste resource. จำ criteria ให้ได้ → image เฉพาะ intermediate/high risk.

PEARL 4 — MOMS Trial: 40% vs 82% Shunt Rate
MOMS trial (Adzick NEJM 2011;364:993): RCT prenatal vs postnatal repair; prenatal = 40% shunt rate vs postnatal = 82% at 12 months. อ่านตัวเลขนี้ให้ขึ้นใจ — ถ้า attending ถามตอนเช้าก็ตอบได้เลย. แต่จำ limitation ด้วย: ↑ preterm delivery; uterine dehiscence; limited centers. MOMS II ไม่พบ diff ใน Vineland composite score ที่ school age แต่ prenatal repair ยังให้ motor benefit + less family burden. Standard of care ยังเป็น postnatal repair.

PEARL 5 — Apnea + Bradycardia ใน MMC Baby = Hydrocephalus/Chiari II จนกว่าจะพิสูจน์ว่าไม่ใช่
Chiari II malformation (hindbrain herniation) เกิดใน nearly all lumbar MMC; อาการ brainstem compression: nystagmus, apnea, bradycardia, gagging, swallowing difficulties, hemodynamic instability. ถ้า MMC baby apneic หลัง repair → ตรวจ head US ก่อน — hydrocephalus อาจกำลัง ↑. Treat hydrocephalus → อาการ brainstem ควร resolve. ถ้า persist หลัง shunt → Chiari II decompression. Apnea + bradycardia + hemodynamic instability = Cushing triad equivalent → treat fast.
12. Evidence Update — Key Studies

Adzick NS et al (MOMS) N Engl J Med 2011;364:993: RCT 183 patients — prenatal MMC repair: 40% shunt rate at 12 mo vs 82% postnatal; ↑ motor + mental development at 30 mo; hindbrain herniation ↓; stopped early for efficacy; uterine dehiscence risk

Houtrow AJ et al (MOMS II) Pediatrics 2020;145(2): school-age follow-up; Vineland composite score NS between prenatal vs postnatal; prenatal group had reduced negative social/family impact + continued motor benefit
Copp AJ, Greene ND Wiley Interdiscip Rev Dev Biol 2013;2:213: comprehensive review of NTD disorders of neurulation; embryology, pathogenesis, classification — foundational reference
Copp AJ et al Lancet Neurol 2013;12:799: NTDs recent advances, unsolved questions, controversies — pathways (PCP, SHH, BMP, GRHL, RA), genetics, prevention, treatment; MTHFR polymorphisms 1.8× risk
Sewell MJ et al Pediatr Dermatol 2015;32:161: neural tube dysraphism: cutaneous markers and imaging review; basis for high/intermediate/low risk cutaneous stigmata classification
Sarris CE et al Neurosurg Focus 2012;33:E3: lipomyelomeningocele pathology, treatment, outcomes: 10–30% complication rate; 5.8% worse neurologic function post-op; 10–20% retethering
Wilson RD et al J Obstet Gynaecol Can 2014;36:927: prenatal screening, diagnosis, pregnancy management of fetal NTDs; fetal US sensitivity 97%, specificity 100%; MSAFP protocols

Krantz DA et al Clin Lab Med 2016;36:401: screening for open NTDs; MSAFP 2.0–2.5 MoM cutoff; 2.0 MoM: specificity 90% open spina bifida, sensitivity 100% anencephaly; amniocentesis AFP + AChE: 96% detection
Kibar Z et al Clin Genet 2007;71:295: genetic basis of NTDs; folate 0.4 mg/day prevents 70%; high-risk: 4 mg/day; MTHFR polymorphisms; PCP pathway genes VANGL1/2, CELSR1
Hutchins GM et al Pediatr Pathol Lab Med 1996;16:701: acquired spinal cord injury in human fetuses with MMC — two-hit theory evidence; second hit from intrauterine exposure justifies fetal repair
Dewan MC, Wellons JC J Neurosurg Pediatr 2019;24:105: fetal surgery for spina bifida — open vs fetoscopic techniques; evolution of endoscopic approach; learning curve and limitations review
Talamonti G et al J Neurosurg 2007;107(suppl 5):368: MMC long-term neurosurgical treatment + follow-up 202 patients; 60–80% develop hydrocephalus requiring VP shunt
Greene S et al Am J Obstet Gynecol 2016;215:495: mode of delivery and infant neurologic outcomes in MMC; C-section before labor associated with improved motor function at 2 years
Cremer R et al Allergy 1998;53:709: latex allergy in spina bifida — prevention by primary prophylaxis (nonlatex gloves from birth); sensitization avoidable if latex-free environment maintained
McComb JG Childs Nerv Syst 2015;31:1641: practical clinical classification of spinal NTDs — comprehensive classification system; basis for Table 60.1
Pang D J Neurosurg Pediatr 2019;23:537: surgical management of complex spinal cord lipomas — 3 types (dorsal, transitional, caudal); how, why, and when to operate
Charney EB et al Am J Dis Child 1991;145:287: ventriculitis in newborns with MMC — antibiotic prophylaxis efficacy; start within 1h of birth; continue ≥48h post-repair
13. Self-Check Q&A
Q: อธิบาย 4 processes ที่ failure → spinal dysraphism พร้อมตัวอย่าง condition ที่เกิดจากแต่ละ failure

ตอบ: 1. Gastrulation: failure to resolve neuroenteric canal → split cord malformation + neuroenteric cysts. 2. Primary neurulation: neural fold fails to close → open NTDs (MMC, meningocele) — level depends on closure site failure. 3. Disjunction: premature → lipomyelomeningocele; incomplete → dermal sinus. 4. Secondary neurulation: failure → caudal dysraphisms (tethered cord, caudal agenesis) with multi-germ-layer tissues from multipotent tail bud.
Q: จำแนก cutaneous stigmata เป็น high/intermediate/low risk และ imaging ที่ recommend สำหรับแต่ละกลุ่ม

ตอบ: High risk → MRI spine: lipoma, acrochordon, pseudotail, aplasia cutis, dermoid cyst/sinus, IH >2.5 cm, ≥2 stigmata. Intermediate risk: atypical dimple (>5 mm or >2.5 cm from anal verge), IH <2.5 cm, hypertrichosis → US if <6 mo; MRI if >6 mo. Low risk → no imaging (US if very concerned): simple dimple, hyper/hypopigmentation, nevi, port-wine stain, telangiectasias, teratoma.
Q: MSAFP screening: timing, cutoff, sensitivity/specificity; เมื่อไหร่ทำ amniocentesis?

ตอบ: Timing: 15–21 weeks gestation; cutoff 2.0–2.5 MoM. 2.0 MoM: ↑ detection by 10%; specificity 90% for open spina bifida; sensitivity 100% for anencephaly. Amniocentesis: for elevated MSAFP → amniotic fluid AFP + acetylcholinesterase (AChE) confirmation test → detects 96% open spina bifida. Fetal US: sensitivity 97%, specificity 100% with experienced sonographer.
Q: ระบุ motor function ที่ level L1, L3, L5 และ S1 ของ MMC — ทำไมถึง important?

ตอบ: L1: hip flexion; L3: knee extension; L5: extensor hallucis longus; S1: plantar flexion. Important because functional level predicts ambulation potential: L3 and below → community ambulation usually achievable; S1 function = very favorable. Functional level ≠ anatomic level always — exposed neural tissue may retain some function; re-assess after repair when edema resolves.
Q: MOMS trial: primary outcomes, key results, limitations

ตอบ: Design: RCT 183 patients, prenatal (<26 wk) vs postnatal repair. Primary outcomes: fetal/neonatal death; shunt need at 12 months (40% prenatal vs 82% postnatal); mental + motor development at 30 months (prenatal better). Secondary: hindbrain herniation ↓, ambulation ↑ in prenatal. Limitations: stopped early for efficacy → underpowered for some outcomes; ↑ preterm delivery; uterine dehiscence; limited centers; narrow enrollment criteria. MOMS II: no diff Vineland score at school age.
Q: Two-hit theory ของ MMC คืออะไร และ connect กับ fetal repair อย่างไร?

ตอบ: First hit: neurologic injury from failure of neural tube closure itself (structural). Second hit: ongoing injury from neural tissue exposure to amniotic fluid, hydrodynamic pressures, direct trauma throughout gestation (Hutchins 1996). Fetal repair benefit: eliminates second hit by covering neural tissue before ongoing intrauterine damage accumulates → ↓ shunt rate (40% vs 82%), ↑ motor outcomes. แต่ standard of care ยังเป็น postnatal repair เพราะ maternal risk ↑ + limited centers.
Q: ทำไม nonlatex gloves จึงสำคัญมากในทารก MMC และ sensitization เกิดขึ้นได้อย่างไร?

ตอบ: Latex sensitization: MMC infants undergo multiple surgeries (closure, shunts, tethered cord releases) → repeated latex exposure → IgE-mediated sensitization → anaphylaxis risk. Prevention: primary prophylaxis with nonlatex gloves from the first contact (delivery room) prevents initial sensitization (Cremer et al Allergy 1998). Practical: inform ALL providers (NICU, OR, radiology, PT/OT); document 'latex precaution' clearly in chart; family must know for home/school.
Q: อธิบาย 3 types ของ lipoma ใน lipomyelomeningocele และ complication ที่ต้องรู้

ตอบ: Dorsal: lipoma attaches at neural placode level (level of myeloschisis). Transitional: extends beyond placode → conus medullaris, may involve nerve roots. Caudal/chaotic: from caudal end of conus, encases nerve roots. Complications of surgery: 10–30% overall complication rate; 5.8% worse neurologic function post-op; 10–20% retethering → re-operation may be needed. 48% neurologically intact at diagnosis → elective surgery to prevent future tethering.
Q: อาการ Chiari II malformation ใน MMC baby คืออะไร และควร manage อย่างไร?

ตอบ: Chiari II signs (brainstem compression from hindbrain herniation): nystagmus, apnea, bradycardia, gagging, swallowing difficulties, syncope, weakness, hemodynamic instability. Management: apnea/bradycardia as presenting sign → first treat hydrocephalus (VP shunt) — symptoms resolve in many patients after shunt. If persist after shunt → Chiari II decompression surgery. Persistence of symptoms after treatment = poor prognostic sign.
Q: closed spinal dysraphism: indications for urgent vs elective surgery vs observation

ตอบ: Observation (conservative): asymptomatic closed dysraphism with no neurologic deficit; reasonable approach but discuss prophylactic surgery (high risk of eventual deterioration). Elective surgery: standard for confirmed closed dysraphism; particularly lipomyelomeningocele with subcutaneous mass even if neurologically intact (prevent future tethering). Urgent/expedited surgery: active neurologic deficit from tethered cord (motor weakness, bladder/bowel dysfunction progressing); dermal sinus with CNS infection. Key point: preventive surgery → 10–20% retethering anyway; counsel family appropriately.

Day 158 Preview — Fanaroff Ch.61
บทต่อไป: Chapter 61 — Hearing Loss in the Newborn Infant. เตรียมตัว: prevalence of permanent childhood hearing loss (PCHL): 1–3/1,000 newborns; universal newborn hearing screening (UNHS): OAE + AABR — algorithm, refer criteria, timing; etiology (GJB2/connexin 26 = 50% genetic SNHL, CMV, hyperbilirubinemia, ototoxic drugs, aminoglycosides, loop diuretics, cisplatin); risk factors (JCIH 2019 guidelines): NICU stay >5 days, aminoglycoside exposure, IVH, meningitis, hyperbilirubinemia requiring exchange transfusion; audiologic follow-up: 1-3-6 rule (diagnose by 3 mo, amplify by 6 mo); cochlear implant candidacy.
